agent_output: {"domain_analysis":{"business_context":"Evolution of a delivery platform's core domain model from a 1:1 Shipment-to-Recipient relationship to a 1:N relationship, enabling multi-recipient shipments. This is a fundamental structural change to the aggregate root's composition, affecting data models, APIs, partner integrations, and downstream business processes.","bounded_context":"Shipment Management — the core bounded context responsible for modeling shipments, recipients, delivery preferences, and fulfillment lifecycle.","ubiquitous_language":[{"term":"Shipment","definition":"A unit of goods being transported from an origin to one or more delivery destinations. The aggregate root in this bounded context."},{"term":"Recipient","definition":"A person or entity designated to receive a delivery. Identified by name, address, phone number, and (now) individual delivery preferences."},{"term":"Delivery Preferences","definition":"Recipient-level configuration governing how and when delivery should occur (e.g., time windows, signature requirements, safe-place instructions, contact method)."},{"term":"Multi-Recipient Shipment","definition":"A shipment with more than one recipient, where the physical goods may be split or the same package routed to multiple destinations sequentially or in parallel."},{"term":"Primary Recipient","definition":"(Proposed) The default or first recipient on a shipment — important for backward compatibility and for processes that require a single point of contact."},{"term":"Partner API","definition":"External integrations that consume shipment data in the current single-recipient format."}]},"business_rules":[{"id":"BR-1","rule":"A Shipment MUST have at least one Recipient at all times.","invariant":true,"invariant_justification":"A shipment without a recipient has no delivery destination and is physically undeliverable. This is a state integrity constraint — violating it means the shipment entity is corrupt.","testable_assertion":"Creating or updating a Shipment with zero Recipients MUST be rejected with a validation error."},{"id":"BR-2","rule":"Each Recipient on a Shipment MUST have a name, address, and phone number.","invariant":true,"invariant_justification":"These are the minimum fields required to physically complete a delivery — the courier needs to know where to go, who to contact, and how to reach them. Missing any one makes the delivery unexecutable.","testable_assertion":"Adding a Recipient with a null or empty name, address, or phone number MUST be rejected."},{"id":"BR-3","rule":"Each Recipient on a Shipment MAY have distinct delivery preferences.","invariant":false,"invariant_justification":"Delivery preferences are a business policy enhancement, not a data integrity constraint. A recipient without explicit preferences falls back to system defaults.","testable_assertion":"Two Recipients on the same Shipment can have different delivery preference values (e.g., one requires signature, the other allows safe-place)."},{"id":"BR-4","rule":"Existing single-recipient shipments MUST remain fully functional without modification after the model change.","invariant":true,"invariant_justification":"2 million production records represent fulfilled or in-progress business commitments. Corrupting or invalidating them breaks state integrity.","testable_assertion":"All 2M existing shipments query and display correctly after migration, each showing exactly one Recipient with all original data intact."},{"id":"BR-5","rule":"External partner APIs MUST continue to receive shipment data in a format they can process.","invariant":false,"invariant_justification":"This is a contractual and operational requirement but not a data integrity invariant — partners could eventually migrate. However, breaking it has severe business consequences.","testable_assertion":"Partner API payloads for single-recipient shipments are identical in structure to the current format. Multi-recipient shipments are either sent as multiple payloads or via a negotiated new format."},{"id":"BR-6","rule":"Shipment status and tracking must be coherent when a shipment has multiple recipients with different delivery outcomes.","invariant":true,"invariant_justification":"If one recipient's delivery succeeds and another fails, the aggregate shipment state must not be corrupt or contradictory. A shipment cannot be simultaneously 'delivered' and 'failed' without a clear resolution model.","testable_assertion":"A Shipment with Recipients A (delivered) and B (failed) has a well-defined aggregate status (e.g., 'partially_delivered') rather than an undefined or contradictory state."},{"id":"BR-7","rule":"Each Recipient on a Shipment MUST be independently addressable for tracking, notifications, and proof-of-delivery.","invariant":true,"invariant_justification":"If recipients share a single tracking/notification channel, delivery confirmation for one could be confused with another — this is data integrity for the fulfillment lifecycle.","testable_assertion":"Each Recipient has a unique delivery record with its own tracking events, notification history, and proof-of-delivery."}],"acceptance_criteria":[{"id":"AC-1","given":"A new Shipment is being created","when":"The creator specifies two or more Recipients with valid name, address, phone, and delivery preferences","then":"The Shipment is created successfully with all Recipients associated and individually queryable"},{"id":"AC-2","given":"A new Shipment is being created","when":"The creator specifies zero Recipients","then":"The system rejects the creation with a clear validation error"},{"id":"AC-3","given":"An existing single-recipient Shipment created before the model change","when":"The Shipment is queried via the API","then":"The Shipment returns correctly with its one Recipient, and all fields (name, address, phone) are intact"},{"id":"AC-4","given":"An external partner API integration is active","when":"A single-recipient Shipment is sent to the partner","then":"The payload format is backward-compatible with the current contract"},{"id":"AC-5","given":"A multi-recipient Shipment is in transit","when":"Delivery succeeds for Recipient A but fails for Recipient B","then":"The Shipment reflects a partial delivery state, and each Recipient has an independent delivery status"},{"id":"AC-6","given":"A multi-recipient Shipment exists","when":"A Recipient is removed and it is the last one","then":"The removal is rejected — a Shipment must always have at least one Recipient"},{"id":"AC-7","given":"A multi-recipient Shipment exists","when":"Each Recipient has different delivery preferences (e.g., one requires signature, another allows safe-place)","then":"The courier/system respects each Recipient's individual preferences independently"},{"id":"AC-8","given":"An existing Shipment with one Recipient","when":"A second Recipient is added after creation","then":"The Shipment now has two Recipients, and both are independently trackable with their own delivery status"}],"edge_cases":[{"scenario":"Multi-recipient shipment where two recipients have the same address","expected_behavior":"Both recipients are treated as distinct entities with separate delivery records, even at the same address. The system does not merge or deduplicate."},{"scenario":"Partner API receives a multi-recipient shipment but only supports single-recipient format","expected_behavior":"The system must have a defined strategy: either fan-out into N single-recipient payloads, designate a primary recipient for the partner, or reject the multi-recipient shipment for that partner channel. This must be an explicit business decision, not a silent data loss."},{"scenario":"Recipient is removed from a multi-recipient shipment mid-transit","expected_behavior":"Only allowed if delivery for that recipient has not yet been attempted. If delivery is in progress or completed, removal is rejected. The minimum-one-recipient invariant must still hold."},{"scenario":"Data migration of 2M existing shipments","expected_behavior":"Existing single-recipient data is migrated to the new 1:N structure with zero data loss. Each existing shipment has exactly one Recipient record post-migration. Migration must be reversible or at minimum validated row-by-row."},{"scenario":"Notifications for a multi-recipient shipment","expected_behavior":"Each recipient receives only notifications relevant to their delivery, not all recipients' events. Recipient B should not get 'delivered' notification when Recipient A's package arrives."},{"scenario":"Shipment cost calculation with multiple recipients","expected_behavior":"Pricing model must account for multiple destinations. Is it one shipment fee with surcharges per recipient, or N separate delivery fees? This is a business decision that must be explicit."},{"scenario":"All recipients on a shipment refuse delivery","expected_behavior":"Shipment enters a return-to-sender or exception state. The aggregate status must handle total delivery failure distinctly from partial delivery."}],"business_impact":{"financial":"Significant. Multi-recipient shipments could increase revenue per shipment but require a new pricing model. Migration costs are non-trivial for 2M records. Partner API changes may require renegotiation of contracts. Incorrect migration risks billing errors on existing shipments.","operational":"High complexity increase. Courier dispatch logic must handle multiple stops per shipment. Status tracking becomes multi-dimensional. Support teams need training on partial-delivery states. Monitoring and alerting must adapt to new aggregate statuses.","user":"Positive for customers who need split deliveries (e.g., gifts to multiple addresses, business distribution). However, the tracking UX becomes more complex — customers need to see per-recipient status clearly. Poor UX here will increase support volume.","risk":"High. Core domain model change touching the aggregate root. Risks include: data migration failure on 2M records, partner API breakage causing fulfillment gaps, introduction of undefined shipment states (partial delivery), and regression in single-recipient flows that represent 100% of current volume.","stakeholders_affected":[{"group":"End Customers","impact":"Gain multi-recipient capability but may face UX complexity. Existing customers must see zero disruption to current single-recipient shipments."},{"group":"Courier / Drivers","impact":"Route planning and delivery workflow changes. Must handle multiple stops per shipment and record independent proof-of-delivery per recipient."},{"group":"External Partners","impact":"API contract changes. Partners on the current single-recipient format will break unless backward compatibility is maintained or migration is coordinated."},{"group":"Customer Support","impact":"New states (partial delivery) and more complex shipment structures increase support complexity. Training required."},{"group":"Finance / Billing","impact":"Pricing model must be defined for multi-recipient shipments. Revenue recognition per shipment changes."},{"group":"Engineering","impact":"Schema migration for 2M records, API versioning, backward compatibility layer, new aggregate status logic, expanded test surface."}]},"confidence":{"level":52,"reasoning":"The product team's requirement is clear in intent (1:N recipients with individual preferences) but critically underspecified in several areas: no definition of what 'delivery preferences' includes, no guidance on partner API migration strategy, no pricing model for multi-recipient shipments, and no definition of aggregate shipment status when recipients have divergent delivery outcomes. These are not contradictions but genuine ambiguities that could be resolved multiple valid ways.","high_confidence_areas":["The 1:N relationship structure and minimum-one-recipient invariant are clear","Backward compatibility for existing 2M shipments is a non-negotiable requirement","Each recipient needs independent tracking and delivery status","Partner API backward compatibility is critical"],"low_confidence_areas":["Exact composition of 'delivery preferences' — what fields are included","Pricing model for multi-recipient shipments","Partner API strategy for multi-recipient payloads — fan-out vs new format vs primary recipient","Whether multi-recipient means multiple physical packages or one package with multiple stops","Aggregate shipment status model for partial delivery scenarios"],"assumptions":["Delivery preferences include at minimum: time window, signature requirement, and safe-place instructions","Multi-recipient means multiple delivery destinations, not multiple people at one address","The data migration will move from a flat recipient-on-shipment structure to a separate recipients collection/table","Partner APIs are versioned or can be extended without breaking existing integrations"]},"escalations":[{"type":"ambiguity","description":"'Delivery preferences' is undefined. The spec says each Recipient can have different delivery preferences but never enumerates what those preferences are. This affects schema design, validation rules, and the courier workflow. Options range from a minimal set (time window, signature required) to an extensive one (contact method, alternate recipient, safe-place instructions, delivery attempt limits).","affected_stakeholders":["Engineering","Product","Courier / Drivers"],"options":["Define a minimal preference set (time window, signature, safe-place) and expand later","Define a flexible key-value preference model","Require Product to enumerate all preferences before implementation"],"recommendation":"Require Product to enumerate the initial set of delivery preferences with explicit field definitions before implementation begins. A flexible schema can accommodate future additions, but the initial set must be concrete."},{"type":"ambiguity","description":"Physical fulfillment model for multi-recipient shipments is unspecified. Does one Shipment become multiple packages (one per recipient), or is it a single package with sequential delivery stops? This fundamentally changes logistics, costing, and status tracking.","affected_stakeholders":["Courier / Drivers","Finance / Billing","Engineering"],"options":["One shipment = multiple packages, each independently routed to a recipient","One shipment = one package making multiple stops in sequence","Support both models with a shipment-level configuration"],"recommendation":"Clarify with Product and Operations. The physical model dictates the entire downstream design — routing, costing, status. Cannot proceed without this."},{"type":"ambiguity","description":"Partner API migration strategy is unspecified. External partners currently receive single-recipient payloads. With multi-recipient shipments, the system must either fan-out into N payloads, send a new multi-recipient format (requiring partner coordination), or designate a primary recipient. Each approach has different business and technical implications.","affected_stakeholders":["External Partners","Engineering","Product"],"options":["Fan-out: send N independent payloads per shipment, one per recipient","New format: version the API and coordinate partner migration","Primary recipient: always send the first recipient to partners, suppressing others"],"recommendation":"Fan-out is the safest short-term approach for backward compatibility, but requires deduplication logic on the partner side. Recommend a versioned API strategy with fan-out as the v1 compatibility layer."},{"type":"ambiguity","description":"Aggregate shipment status model for multi-recipient scenarios is undefined. When recipients have divergent outcomes (one delivered, one failed), what is the shipment's status? There is no industry standard — this is a business policy decision that affects reporting, SLAs, and customer communication.","affected_stakeholders":["Customer Support","End Customers","Finance / Billing"],"options":["Introduce 'partially_delivered' as a new shipment status","Shipment status = worst-case across recipients (any failure = shipment failed)","Shipment status = best-case across recipients (any success = shipment delivered)","Decouple: shipment has no aggregate delivery status, only recipient-level statuses"],"recommendation":"Introduce 'partially_delivered' as a distinct status. It is the most honest representation and avoids misleading customers or support teams. Requires adding a new value to the shipment status enum."}],"rejection_reasons":[]}
